quote: the garage roof would need to be redone because, what's that? Okay. Now you stop this from it's, oh, it's 20 years old.
stewart_read: Meg mentions the roofing program in one sentence and then moves directly to utility company. She never routes the roofing signal — no 'let me flag our roofing team,' no attempt to make roofing feel like a parallel track. The mention lands as a soft reassurance, not a revenue capture. Reactive read: Meg recognized the signal and named the program but did not convert it into a routing action — this is the primary hypothesis. The strategic read — that she intentionally deferred roofing development to keep Kelly moving — is undermined by the remaining eight minutes of the call, in which roofing is never revisited at all. A deliberate deferral would show a follow-through move; none appears.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg deliberately kept roofing brief to avoid overwhelming Kelly with two separate conversations at once, planning to address it at the closer stage. Rejected — no follow-up thread appears in the remaining eight minutes, and the schema explicitly calls for routing to the roofing team in real time.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Meg named the product correctly, which means she knows it exists — the gap is the routing move. Walk her through what a real-time roofing bridge sounds like: something like 'I'm going to put a note on this for our roofing team specifically — they work hand-in-hand with solar on situations like this. You'd get both looked at together.' The manager should confirm with Kenny whether co-routing is the correct action and what that handoff looks like operationally.

quote: I'm assuming it's a single family home and a property owner. Yes. And Kettles.
stewart_read: Meg confirms homeownership and moves on. She never asks who else is on the title. This is not a judgment call — title_co_owners is a required Verify item explicitly because a co-owner who is absent from the appointment is the structural cause of no-shows and late cancellations. Without this, the closer has no way to know whether the person they're presenting to is the sole decision-maker. The button-up at 08:37 has no spouse-coordination spine because Meg never established whether one was needed.
coaching_implication: This is a simple drill gap, not a strategic miss. In the 1-on-1, add one line to Meg's Verify flow right after homeownership confirmation: 'And is anyone else on the title with you?' Takes four seconds. Without it, this appointment is structurally exposed at the closer stage.

quote: our program, what it does is it actually saves you money immediately, and the only requirement is that you have a credit score above 650.
stewart_read: Meg states the credit threshold as 650. The schema records 670 as the active threshold, with a note that SESSION18_fcbf33c1 specifically surfaces this discrepancy. This is a schema-drift instance — not a persuasion failure, but a procedural one. If 670 is the true floor and Meg consistently books at 650, the closer is inheriting leads that may DQ at the credit pull.
alternative_hypothesis: The 650 figure may reflect a current policy update not yet captured in the schema. Kenny's resolution of the credit threshold question (schema section qualifiers.credit_qualifier, 'policy question for Kenny') would resolve this either way.
coaching_implication: Do not coach Meg on this until Kenny clarifies the active threshold. Flag the discrepancy in the schema review for Kenny. Once resolved, if 670 is confirmed, this becomes a brief correction: show Meg the number and make sure her script matches. If 650 is correct, update the schema.

quote: K. Makes sense. Alright. I know summer bills tend to be a lot higher.
stewart_read: Kelly has just revealed a $500 summer bill driven by a pool — a high-load, emotionally concrete pain point. Meg acknowledges it ('I know summer bills tend to be a lot higher') and then asks a clarifying question. She captures the bill data competently. What she does not do is flip it. The utility is the cause of the $500 bill; that's the emotional alignment move the schema calls for. Meg's response is factual and empathetic but stops short of redirecting Kelly's cost frustration toward the utility as the problem solar solves.
coaching_implication: The flip doesn't require fabricating numbers. Kelly handed Meg the number herself — $500. A suggested rephrase: 'Five hundred dollars to Ameren in one month — that's the bill we're trying to make go away.' That one move turns the bill from a data point into a reason to keep moving. Show Meg the difference between capturing the anchor and activating it.

quote: You mentioned you've got the pool. Do you have a hot tub or electric vehicle? Anything like that?
stewart_read: Meg probes for additional load amplifiers immediately after the pool mention — this is good instinct. The pool is already confirmed; the hot-tub and EV probe is Meg looking for further qualification signals that would increase system size and close urgency. Kelly says no to both. The probe itself is worth surfacing as a positive pattern, not a failure. There is no direct schema item for load-amplifier probing, but this is the behavior the verify section's interest_in_solar and average_bill items are trying to generate. Meg arrived at the right move through a different path.
coaching_implication: Surface this as a positive in the 1-on-1 — Meg is thinking about load profile, not just checking boxes. The manager can use this to anchor a conversation about what to do when the probe returns positive: a confirmed hot tub or EV is a reason to explicitly name the system-size implication and increase urgency. Meg found the instinct; the coaching is on what to do with it when the answer is yes.

quote: I'm in I'm in Walmart, and so I don't have any of my stuff.
stewart_read: Kelly discloses she is mobile and away from home. Meg stays on the utility website path — asking Kelly to navigate to Ameren.com and log in from Walmart. The schema's shopping_customer_protocol calls for walking the customer through the utility website on their phone, which is essentially what Meg does. The bill eventually comes through at 07:15. The path took roughly three minutes of navigation friction (04:05–07:15), including a spelling exchange and some good-natured chatter that kept Kelly engaged. Meg managed the friction well relationally; operationally, the time cost was moderate but not severe for a paid lead.
coaching_implication: Not a failure — flag as a positive contrast to SESSION14's 8-15 minute tech-friction spiral. Meg kept Kelly on the line through chit-chat (Jack Daniels exchange) while the navigation completed. In the 1-on-1, acknowledge this as the right move and note that the schema's 2-minute pivot threshold is the guardrail — Meg stayed inside it.

quote: Just had a couple questions to build your solar design and get those options ready for you.
stewart_read: The interest_in_solar question — what attracted Kelly to solar — is never asked across the entire call. This is the emotional anchor item in the Verify section, distinct from the bill-anchor. The schema marks it as required with depth_grading: true and notes that top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Meg never opens the door. The practical consequence: the handoff text and the closer's opening have no personalized emotional thread to pull — no 'you mentioned wanting to offset your pool costs' or 'you said the summer bills were what pushed you.' The bill data exists; the motivation story does not.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, show Meg where this question fits — right after address confirmation, before or alongside utility. The coaching is not just about checking the box. It's about what the answer gives you: a reason to personalize the text follow-up and a thread the closer can open with. Ask Meg: 'What do you know about why Kelly actually wants solar?' She won't have an answer. That's the gap.
